name: Monsanto Full
description: The term 'Monsanto Full' represents a collection of conspiracy theories and criticisms directed at the Monsanto Company (now Bayer after its acquisition). These theories generally accuse Monsanto of a wide range of wrongdoings, including intentionally creating harmful genetically modified organisms (GMOs), manipulating regulatory agencies, suppressing scientific research that contradicts their interests, causing environmental damage, and prioritizing profit over public health and safety. Proponents often cite studies (sometimes misinterpreted or selectively chosen) suggesting negative health effects of GMOs or glyphosate (Roundup), Monsanto's flagship herbicide. They point to Monsanto's aggressive legal actions against farmers saving seeds as evidence of corporate greed and control over the food supply. The origins of these theories can be traced back to the rise of anti-GMO activism in the late 20th and early 21st centuries. Mainstream scientific consensus largely supports the safety of currently approved GMOs, stating that they undergo rigorous testing and regulation. Regulatory agencies like the EPA and FDA also maintain that glyphosate is safe when used as directed. Critics of the 'Monsanto Full' narrative argue that it often relies on misinformation, cherry-picked data, and unsubstantiated claims, contributing to public fear and distrust of science and technology. They emphasize the importance of evidence-based decision-making and scientific consensus in evaluating the safety and benefits of GMOs and agricultural chemicals.
subtitle: Conspiracy theories surrounding Monsanto/Bayer

claims.evidence: Studies linking GMOs to health problems (often disputed); anecdotal evidence.
claims.criticism: Mainstream science supports the safety of approved GMOs; regulatory agencies conduct extensive testing.

claims.evidence: Lobbying efforts; perceived conflicts of interest; revolving door between Monsanto and government.
claims.criticism: Lobbying is a legal activity; regulatory agencies have independent oversight mechanisms.

claims.claim: Glyphosate (Roundup) causes cancer and other health problems.
claims.evidence: IARC classification of glyphosate as 'probably carcinogenic to humans'; some studies linking glyphosate to cancer.
claims.criticism: Other regulatory agencies (EPA, EFSA) have concluded that glyphosate is unlikely to pose a carcinogenic risk to humans at current exposure levels; conflicting scientific evidence.

keyFigures.name: Marion Nestle
keyFigures.role: Nutrition and public health professor; critic of corporate influence on food policy.

keyFigures.name: Kevin Folta
keyFigures.role: Horticultural sciences professor; advocate for GMOs and critic of anti-GMO activism.

mythologyConnections: David vs. Goliath narrative (small farmers vs. large corporation)
Frankenstein's monster (unforeseen consequences of scientific advancement)

timeline.event: IARC classifies glyphosate as 'probably carcinogenic to humans'.

sources.author: Carey Gillam
sources.work: Whitewash: The Story of a Weed Killer, Cancer, and the Corruption of Science
sources.title: Carey Gillam

sources.author: Jon Entine
sources.work: Pandora's Seed: The Unforeseen Cost of America's Food
sources.title: Jon Entine
